summary: This brief post promotes O'Neil's Bloomberg column arguing that mass incarceration, inequality, and racism are root causes of pandemic vulnerability. Drawing on research from Measures for Justice, she contends that these interconnected social ills weakened community immune systems and made both the U.S. and the world more susceptible to COVID-19. The post serves as a gateway to her fuller argument that structural racism isn't just a social justice issue but a public health catastrophe.
key_points: Mass incarceration weakens community immune systems, increasing pandemic vulnerability
Racism, inequality, and incarceration are interconnected causes of the COVID-19 crisis
The research from Measures for Justice provides empirical backing for these claims
These factors affect pandemic outcomes both domestically and globally
money_quotes: Mass incarceration, inequality, and racism cause epidemics both here and worldwide
Mass incarceration and other social ills made the world more vulnerable
key_insight: Structural racism and mass incarceration aren't just social justice issues—they're public health infrastructure failures that amplify pandemics.
